transcript.transcript: I am Pam Northcutt, here with Penny Casey. We are here today visiting with Eldene Wasem, who knew Millard "Dad" Roberts. We are asking her to share some memories of Dad Roberts. Do we have your permission to record you for America 250? Yes you do. In 1953, the rural town of Weiser, Idaho, held a regional fiddle contest that quickly developed into one of the most important concerts in the world. The National Old Time Fiddle contest. This contest continues to this day. Kentucky born an aging current Harpster, Idaho resident, Millard Dad Roberts won this very first contest. Eldene, can I ask you how old you are? 97. So, do you know any part of Dad Roberts backstory? We didn't know much until I met a nephew. Uh, dad Roberts was a very private man and nobody ever asked him anything. He was just Dad Roberts, and we accepted it. But I do know he was born in Clay County, Kentucky, came to Oregon, and he did a variety of things. He said, I won the Bronco rider contest. But I tried to break one and it broke me. It ended up dragging me, and he carried the scars to death. He logged. He worked in the fruit and I gathered it was Dayton, Idaho, but I'm not sure. (This would more than likely be Dayton Washington). And finally, he was quite a wanderer. He seemed to be a restless guy. And I don't know how he found Harpster Idaho, but the calling of gold. He thought it could be a prospector. And, uh, Whitman had abandoned a barn not far from our house, the Harpster on Green Creek. And that's where he settled. He cleaned the barn out. I think he cleaned it out and, um, used the stalls to live in. And that was his home until he moved up on the South Fork River. So was that in approximately 1931? Yes. Now, did he have family? Did he have wife and children? Uh, he had a wife, Polly. And I kind of think she went to Oregon with him and may have passed away some things. Is a mystery,of dad. But he did have one daughter, Annie Laurie. And, um, she came to Harpster at one time and my sister Lorraine became friends with her. But I'm sorry to say I never met her. And how old was Dad Roberts at the time he arrived in Harpster? 63. Oh, we got it! I keep mixing all of these things up. We're great. And how old were you when he arrived in Harpster? Five. Okay. And where did he live on Green Creek. In this old barn that the miner Whitman left. And I don't know whether there was a mine close there. But on the one up South Fork, there was. And so he moved next to the South Fork on the Clearwater River? Yes. And the his mine was directly behind the cabin. And to my knowledge, he built that cabin. And the cabin was literally right on the right on the riverbank? Yes, absolutely. And what was his mode of travel? He walked everywhere. He had nothing. I don't even think he maybe he had a wheelbarrow and his boat. But the only time he used his boat was across the river. Okay. So winter, spring. He used the boat year round. Even high water? Uh, to my knowledge. Okay. You know, I wasn't a very old child at the time. And so a lot of it is memory of how good things were. How did he carry his fiddle when he was afoot? He wrapped it in a newspaper, put in a gunny sack and threw it over his shoulder with his treats that he brought everybody. Okay. And what did he look like? And how did he dress? Um, he dressed very much like an old timer. He wore Levi's. He was very short, so he had to roll the Levi' up. Um, and but if he dressed up, he had a white shirt, a vest that he always wore, and a red bandana that he put around his neck. Do you remember his favorite song? Annie Laurie. Because of his, that's who he named his daughter after. And what have you heard about his temperament or what do you recall? Um, what I remember he was very sweet, loving, giving man. But some of the neighbors had seen him in another mode, and he had a very volatile temper. And when he would get mad, he would take his hat, throw it on the ground, jump up and down on it and flatten it out and have a string of cusswords. Well, his name was Millard. But where do you think the Dad Roberts came from? Did anybody call him Millard? No, no, he was Dad Roberts in the Harpster area. And I think even at the old fiddle contest, he was Dad Roberts. Yeah. So was he a good neighbor? How was he with your the community in Harpster? He was very good. If anybody needed help, like the day, my brother, they all gathered to do a housewarming. Dad Roberts was there. He wasn't a real good carpenter, but he was certainly there to help and always remembered kid's birthday and people. So he won the National Old Time Fiddle Championships in 1953. Have you ever attended the Old Time Fiddle Festival? Yes, Harold and I went down. Okay. And what caused his death? Um, nobody's really sure. Other than we know he drowned. He had his mail in the boat, so we know he rowed across to pick up mail. And, um, when they found him, his cat was waiting on the bank for him, and he was between two rocks. So, uh, he either fell or had a heart attack. And my understanding that he drowned in 1954, in high water in the spring. And he was 85 years old. Is that correct? That's correct. And do you know where he's buried? Yes. Close to the offices of the Grangeville Cemetery. And is his cabin still there on the south? I think just boards and the old apple tree. I have seen it several springs. And what's your fondest memory of Dad Roberts? Can you tell the cantaloupe story? I what was the second question? Can you tell about the cantaloupe story? Oh, yes. He raised a big garden and he raised a cantaloupe and watermelon because he was friends to everybody in the neighborhood. But he had a rabbit that he detested, and he called it that old Blue Rabbit. And it wasn't a rabbit. It was the Old Blue Rabbit. And he and that rabbit just couldn't come to terms. And so we decided he was going to poison it. So he plugged the cantaloupes and put poison in. And he thought he'd be a nice neighbor. He grabbed a cantaloupe and took it to the Rupe Family. Well, we as children would see him coming and meet him. Well, the second time that day, here came Dad Roberts again. Well, that was very unusual because it wasn't a short walk. It was probably about a mile and a half. And when we got to him, he was just hot and sweaty and panicky. He didn't want to talk to us, which was unusual. He wanted to talk to my mother and talk to her now. So we ran and got our mother, and she said, have you? He asked her, have you eaten that cantaloupe? No, we haven't eaten yet. Oh thank goodness! He was just so relieved. And my mother couldn't understand why. So he told her he had poisoned Old Blue Rabbit and got the wrong cantaloupe. So, we never heard any more about old Blue Rabbit and the cantaloupe. So I don't know whether he and cantaloupe story. He. Whether he gave it or poisoned it or what. What else did he bring in the gunny sack to the children. Oh, fudge. And it was grainy and had a few whiskers, but we ate up with gusto. We loved it. We thought it was the best thing. The other thing I remember was he raised bees and he brought honeycombs to us, which we loved. Well, thank you for sharing your kind memories. Yes.

lines.line: # Introduction and Permission to Record

> The interview begins with an introduction of El-deen Wasim, who knew Millard 'Dad' Roberts, and a request for her permission to record the conversation for America 250. She grants permission, setting the stage for the oral history session.

metadata.title: Introduction and Permission to Record
metadata.synopsis: The interview begins with an introduction of El-deen Wasim, who knew Millard 'Dad' Roberts, and a request for her permission to record the conversation for America 250. She grants permission, setting the stage for the oral history session.

lines.line: # The National Old Time Fiddle Contest and Dad Roberts' Win

> The speaker recounts the origins of the National Old Time Fiddle contest in Weiser, Idaho, which began in 1953 and became a significant event. Millard 'Dad' Roberts, originally from Kentucky and residing in Harpster, Idaho, won the very first contest, establishing his legacy in the world of old-time fiddling.

metadata.title: The National Old Time Fiddle Contest and Dad Roberts' Win
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recounts the origins of the National Old Time Fiddle contest in Weiser, Idaho, which began in 1953 and became a significant event. Millard 'Dad' Roberts, originally from Kentucky and residing in Harpster, Idaho, won the very first contest, establishing his legacy in the world of old-time fiddling.

lines.line: # Dad Roberts' Backstory and Life Journey

> The speaker shares what she knows about Dad Roberts' background, noting his private nature and limited information. She explains that he was born in Clay County, Kentucky, traveled to Oregon, and worked various jobs including logging and fruit picking. He was a wanderer, eventually drawn to Harpster, Idaho by the prospect of gold, where he settled in an abandoned barn before moving to a cabin by the South Fork River.

metadata.title: Dad Roberts' Backstory and Life Journey
metadata.synopsis: The speaker shares what she knows about Dad Roberts' background, noting his private nature and limited information. She explains that he was born in Clay County, Kentucky, traveled to Oregon, and worked various jobs including logging and fruit picking. He was a wanderer, eventually drawn to Harpster, Idaho by the prospect of gold, where he settled in an abandoned barn before moving to a cabin by the South Fork River.

lines.line: # Arrival in Harpster, Family, and Early Life There

> The discussion focuses on the approximate year Dad Roberts arrived in Harpster, Idaho, which was around 1931. The speaker describes his family, mentioning his wife Polly and daughter Annie Laurie, and details his living arrangements in a barn and later a cabin near the river and a mine. The speaker also shares her own age and arrival in Harpster, providing context for their early years in the area.

metadata.title: Arrival in Harpster, Family, and Early Life There
metadata.synopsis: The discussion focuses on the approximate year Dad Roberts arrived in Harpster, Idaho, which was around 1931. The speaker describes his family, mentioning his wife Polly and daughter Annie Laurie, and details his living arrangements in a barn and later a cabin near the river and a mine. The speaker also shares her own age and arrival in Harpster, providing context for their early years in the area.

lines.line: # Dad Roberts' Lifestyle and Daily Life

> The speaker describes Dad Roberts' simple lifestyle, noting that he traveled everywhere on foot and used a boat only to cross the river. He carried his fiddle wrapped in newspaper inside a gunny sack, and dressed in rolled-up Levi's, a white shirt, vest, and red bandana. These details paint a picture of his daily habits and resourcefulness.

metadata.title: Dad Roberts' Lifestyle and Daily Life
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes Dad Roberts' simple lifestyle, noting that he traveled everywhere on foot and used a boat only to cross the river. He carried his fiddle wrapped in newspaper inside a gunny sack, and dressed in rolled-up Levi's, a white shirt, vest, and red bandana. These details paint a picture of his daily habits and resourcefulness.

lines.line: # Personality, Temperament, and Community Relations

> The speaker recalls Dad Roberts as a sweet, loving, and generous man, though some neighbors witnessed his volatile temper. She shares a vivid anecdote about his anger, describing how he would stomp on his hat and curse when upset. This section highlights both his kindness and his fiery side.

metadata.title: Personality, Temperament, and Community Relations
metadata.synopsis: The speaker recalls Dad Roberts as a sweet, loving, and generous man, though some neighbors witnessed his volatile temper. She shares a vivid anecdote about his anger, describing how he would stomp on his hat and curse when upset. This section highlights both his kindness and his fiery side.

lines.line: # The Name 'Dad Roberts' and His Identity

> The conversation turns to the origin of the nickname 'Dad Roberts.' The speaker explains that he was universally known as Dad Roberts in Harpster and even at the fiddle contest, with no one calling him by his given name, Millard. This underscores his unique identity within the community.

metadata.title: The Name 'Dad Roberts' and His Identity
metadata.synopsis: The conversation turns to the origin of the nickname 'Dad Roberts.' The speaker explains that he was universally known as Dad Roberts in Harpster and even at the fiddle contest, with no one calling him by his given name, Millard. This underscores his unique identity within the community.

lines.line: # His Role as a Neighbor and Community Member

> The speaker describes Dad Roberts as a helpful and caring neighbor, always willing to assist others, such as during housewarmings. He was remembered for celebrating children's birthdays and being present for community events, demonstrating his strong ties to those around him.

metadata.title: His Role as a Neighbor and Community Member
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes Dad Roberts as a helpful and caring neighbor, always willing to assist others, such as during housewarmings. He was remembered for celebrating children's birthdays and being present for community events, demonstrating his strong ties to those around him.

lines.line: # The Fiddle Contest and Festival Attendance

> The speaker confirms Dad Roberts' victory at the National Old Time Fiddle Championships in 1953 and briefly mentions her own attendance at the festival, connecting personal experience to the historical event.

metadata.title: The Fiddle Contest and Festival Attendance
metadata.synopsis: The speaker confirms Dad Roberts' victory at the National Old Time Fiddle Championships in 1953 and briefly mentions her own attendance at the festival, connecting personal experience to the historical event.

lines.line: # Dad Roberts' Death and Burial

> The circumstances of Dad Roberts' death are discussed, with the speaker noting that he drowned in high water in the spring of 1954 at the age of 85. She describes how he was found with his mail and his cat waiting on the bank, and mentions his burial near the offices of the Grangeville Cemetery.

metadata.title: Dad Roberts' Death and Burial
metadata.synopsis: The circumstances of Dad Roberts' death are discussed, with the speaker noting that he drowned in high water in the spring of 1954 at the age of 85. She describes how he was found with his mail and his cat waiting on the bank, and mentions his burial near the offices of the Grangeville Cemetery.

lines.line: # The Fate of His Cabin and Legacy

> The speaker reflects on the current state of Dad Roberts' cabin, noting that only boards and an old apple tree remain. She mentions having seen the site several times in the spring, indicating the lasting physical traces of his life in the area.

metadata.title: The Fate of His Cabin and Legacy
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the current state of Dad Roberts' cabin, noting that only boards and an old apple tree remain. She mentions having seen the site several times in the spring, indicating the lasting physical traces of his life in the area.

lines.line: # Fond Memories and the Cantaloupe Story

> The speaker shares her fondest memory of Dad Roberts, recounting the humorous and dramatic cantaloupe story involving his attempt to poison a troublesome blue rabbit. She describes how he mistakenly gave a poisoned cantaloupe to her family, his panic upon realizing the error, and the relief when they hadn't eaten it. The story illustrates his relationships with neighbors and his quirky personality.

metadata.title: Fond Memories and the Cantaloupe Story
metadata.synopsis: The speaker shares her fondest memory of Dad Roberts, recounting the humorous and dramatic cantaloupe story involving his attempt to poison a troublesome blue rabbit. She describes how he mistakenly gave a poisoned cantaloupe to her family, his panic upon realizing the error, and the relief when they hadn't eaten it. The story illustrates his relationships with neighbors and his quirky personality.

lines.line: # Gifts to Children and Final Thoughts

> The speaker recalls the treats Dad Roberts brought to children, including homemade fudge and honeycombs from his bees. She describes the children's enthusiasm for these gifts, despite their rustic appearance, and the interview concludes with gratitude for sharing these memories.

metadata.title: Gifts to Children and Final Thoughts

Millard 'Dad' Roberts, originally from Kentucky and residing in Harpster, Idaho, won the very first contest, establishing his legacy in the world of old-time fiddling.
# Dad Roberts' Backstory and Life Journey

> The speaker shares what she knows about Dad Roberts' background, noting his private nature and limited information. She explains that he was born in Clay County, Kentucky, traveled to Oregon, and worked various jobs including logging and fruit picking. He was a wanderer, eventually drawn to Harpster, Idaho by the prospect of gold, where he settled in an abandoned barn before moving to a cabin by the South Fork River.

> The discussion focuses on the approximate year Dad Roberts arrived in Harpster, Idaho, which was around 1931. The speaker also shares her own age and arrival in Harpster, providing context for their early years in the area.
# Dad Roberts' Lifestyle and Daily Life

> The speaker describes Dad Roberts' simple lifestyle, noting that he traveled everywhere on foot and used a boat only to cross the river. He carried his fiddle wrapped in newspaper inside a gunny sack, and dressed in rolled-up Levi's, a white shirt, vest, and red bandana. These details paint a picture of his daily habits and resourcefulness.

> The speaker recalls Dad Roberts as a sweet, loving, and generous man, though some neighbors witnessed his volatile temper. She shares a vivid anecdote about his anger, describing how he would stomp on his hat and curse when upset. This section highlights both his kindness and his fiery side.
# The Name 'Dad Roberts' and His Identity

> The conversation turns to the origin of the nickname 'Dad Roberts.' The speaker explains that he was universally known as Dad Roberts in Harpster and even at the fiddle contest, with no one calling him by his given name, Millard. This underscores his unique identity within the community.

> The circumstances of Dad Roberts' death are discussed, with the speaker noting that he drowned in high water in the spring of 1954 at the age of 85. She describes how he was found with his mail and his cat waiting on the bank, and mentions his burial near the offices of the Grangeville Cemetery.

story.title: Eldene Wasem - Memories of Millard "Dad"Roberts on the S.F. Clearwater River
story.description: The interview features Eldene Wasem sharing her memories of Millard "Dad" Roberts, a notable fiddler and wanderer who won the first National Old Time Fiddle Contest in Weiser, Idaho, in 1953. She recalls his enigmatic past, his life in Harpster, Idaho, and his modest lifestyle, traversing the area on foot and using a boat to cross rivers. Roberts is remembered for his kind demeanor but also for having a volatile temper. The narrative includes a humorous story involving a cantaloupe and his encounters with a troublesome rabbit, highlighting his generosity to neighbors and local children. Roberts tragically drowned in 1954, aged 85.
